title_en: The Correct Formulas for Sending Blessings
title_id: Redaksi yang Benar dalam Membaca Shalawat
content_en:
The question of how to send blessings upon the Prophet — which specific formulas to use — is addressed directly by the Companions of the Prophet, who asked him: 'O Messenger of Allah, we know how to greet you, but how should we send blessings upon you?' The Prophet's response to this question established the specific formulas of salawat that have been used by the Muslim Ummah in the prayer and in other contexts across fourteen centuries — formulas that represent the divinely guided and Prophetically endorsed language for this act of worship.

The Ibrahimi Salawat — named for its invocation of the blessing upon Ibrahim — is the most comprehensive and most highly recommended formula, taught by the Prophet himself and incorporated into every Islamic prayer as the salawat of the tashahud. The most complete form is: 'Allahumma salli 'ala Muhammadin wa 'ala ali Muhammadin, kama sallayta 'ala Ibrahima wa 'ala ali Ibrahim, innaka Hamidun Majid. Allahumma barik 'ala Muhammadin wa 'ala ali Muhammadin, kama barakta 'ala Ibrahima wa 'ala ali Ibrahim, innaka Hamidun Majid.' The translation is: 'O Allah, send blessings upon Muhammad and upon the family of Muhammad, as You sent blessings upon Ibrahim and upon the family of Ibrahim — verily, You are Praiseworthy and Glorious. O Allah, bless Muhammad and the family of Muhammad, as You blessed Ibrahim and the family of Ibrahim — verily, You are Praiseworthy and Glorious.'

The theological structure of this formula is remarkable. The supplicant asks Allah to bless the Prophet in a manner that parallels His blessing of Ibrahim — and Ibrahim is specifically chosen because he is the most universally honored of all prophets, the progenitor of the Abrahamic religious tradition, and the friend (khalil) of Allah. By invoking the blessing upon Muhammad in comparison to that upon Ibrahim, the salawat acknowledges the Prophet's supreme prophetic status while using the most elevated precedent in the prophetic tradition as the measure of the requested blessing. The divine names used at the close — Hamid (Praiseworthy) and Majid (Glorious) — are specifically chosen for their connection to the name Muhammad (from the root h-m-d, meaning praise) and to the divine majesty that the salawat invokes.

Simpler formulas are also permissible and rewarded, including the brief: 'Salla Allahu 'alayhi wa sallam' (May Allah send blessings and peace upon him) — commonly written as SAW or (peace be upon him) in English — which is the minimum salawat uttered by Muslims at every mention of the Prophet's name. Al-Jahdhami notes that while the simpler formula is valid and rewarded, the more complete Ibrahimi Salawat carries greater reward and should be used at the more formal and deliberate occasions of salawat practice.

The scholars have discussed whether it is permissible to use formulas of salawat not explicitly transmitted from the Prophet — formulas composed by later scholars and Sufi masters — in addition to the Prophetically transmitted formulas. The general scholarly consensus is that supplementary formulas are permissible so long as they do not contain theological errors or exaggerations that would conflict with Islamic monotheism. The Prophetically transmitted formulas should always retain their primacy, particularly in the prayer.

The pronunciation of the salawat with genuine engagement of the heart — not merely as a verbal formula mechanically recited — is consistently emphasized by the scholars as the most important dimension of the salawat's quality. The person who sends blessings upon the Prophet while genuinely reflecting on the Prophet's greatness, their own love for him, and their gratitude for his sacrifices in conveying the divine message is performing the salawat in its fullest and most spiritually productive sense. The words and the meaning should unite in a single act of sincere, love-driven worship.
content_id:
Pertanyaan tentang bagaimana membaca shalawat kepada Nabi — redaksi spesifik mana yang harus digunakan — dijawab langsung oleh para sahabat Nabi yang bertanya kepada beliau: "Wahai Rasulullah, kami tahu cara memberi salam kepadamu, tetapi bagaimana kami harus membaca shalawat untukmu?" Respons Nabi terhadap pertanyaan ini menetapkan redaksi shalawat yang telah digunakan oleh umat Muslim dalam shalat dan dalam konteks-konteks lainnya selama empat belas abad — redaksi yang merepresentasikan bahasa yang dibimbing secara ilahi dan disetujui secara kenabian untuk amalan ibadah ini.

Shalawat Ibrahimiyyah — dinamai berdasarkan penyebutannya tentang berkah kepada Ibrahim — adalah redaksi yang paling komprehensif dan paling sangat dianjurkan, yang diajarkan oleh Nabi sendiri dan dimasukkan ke dalam setiap shalat Islam sebagai shalawat dalam tasyahud. Bentuk paling lengkapnya adalah: "Allahumma shalli 'ala Muhammadin wa 'ala ali Muhammadin, kama shallayta 'ala Ibrahima wa 'ala ali Ibrahim, innaka Hamidun Majid. Allahumma barik 'ala Muhammadin wa 'ala ali Muhammadin, kama barakta 'ala Ibrahima wa 'ala ali Ibrahim, innaka Hamidun Majid." Artinya: "Ya Allah, limpahkanlah shalawat kepada Muhammad dan kepada keluarga Muhammad, sebagaimana Engkau telah melimpahkan shalawat kepada Ibrahim dan kepada keluarga Ibrahim — sungguh, Engkau Maha Terpuji lagi Maha Mulia. Ya Allah, berkahilah Muhammad dan keluarga Muhammad, sebagaimana Engkau telah memberkahi Ibrahim dan keluarga Ibrahim — sungguh, Engkau Maha Terpuji lagi Maha Mulia."

Struktur teologis redaksi ini sungguh luar biasa. Orang yang berdoa memohon kepada Allah agar memberkati Nabi dengan cara yang sejajar dengan pemberkatan-Nya kepada Ibrahim — dan Ibrahim dipilih secara khusus karena ia adalah nabi yang paling dimuliakan secara universal, leluhur tradisi agama Abrahamik, dan kekasih (khalil) Allah. Dengan menyerukan berkah kepada Muhammad sejajar dengan berkah kepada Ibrahim, shalawat ini mengakui kedudukan kenabian Nabi yang tertinggi sekaligus menggunakan preseden paling mulia dalam tradisi kenabian sebagai tolok ukur berkah yang dimohonkan. Nama-nama ilahi yang digunakan di akhir — Hamid (Maha Terpuji) dan Majid (Maha Mulia) — dipilih secara khusus karena kaitannya dengan nama Muhammad (dari akar h-m-d yang berarti pujian) dan dengan keagungan ilahi yang diinvokasi oleh shalawat ini.

Redaksi yang lebih sederhana juga diperbolehkan dan berpahala, termasuk ungkapan singkat: "Shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam" — yang merupakan shalawat minimum yang diucapkan oleh umat Muslim setiap kali nama Nabi disebut. Al-Jahdhami mencatat bahwa meskipun redaksi yang lebih sederhana sah dan berpahala, Shalawat Ibrahimiyyah yang lebih lengkap membawa pahala yang lebih besar dan sebaiknya digunakan pada kesempatan-kesempatan shalawat yang lebih formal dan disengaja.

Para ulama telah membahas apakah diperbolehkan menggunakan redaksi shalawat yang tidak secara eksplisit diriwayatkan dari Nabi — redaksi yang disusun oleh ulama-ulama belakangan dan para guru Sufi — selain redaksi yang diriwayatkan dari Nabi. Konsensus ulama secara umum adalah bahwa redaksi tambahan diperbolehkan selama tidak mengandung kesalahan teologis atau berlebih-lebihan yang akan bertentangan dengan tauhid Islam. Redaksi yang diriwayatkan dari Nabi harus selalu mempertahankan primasi-nya, khususnya dalam shalat.

Pengucapan shalawat dengan keterlibatan hati yang tulus — bukan sekadar redaksi verbal yang diucapkan secara mekanis — secara konsisten ditekankan oleh para ulama sebagai dimensi terpenting dari kualitas shalawat. Orang yang membaca shalawat kepada Nabi sambil benar-benar merenungkan kebesaran Nabi, kecintaan mereka kepada beliau, dan rasa syukur mereka atas pengorbanannya dalam menyampaikan pesan ilahi, sedang melaksanakan shalawat dalam arti yang paling penuh dan paling produktif secara spiritual. Kata-kata dan maknanya harus bersatu dalam satu amalan ibadah yang tulus dan didorong oleh cinta.
content_ar: The question of how to send البركات upon النبي ﷺ — which specific formulas to use — is addressed directly by the الصحابة of النبي ﷺ, who asked him: 'O Messenger of الله, we know how to greet you, but how should we send البركات upon you?' النبي ﷺ's response to this question established the specific formulas of salawat that have been used by the مسلم الأمة in the الصلاة and in other contexts across fourteen centuries — formulas that represent the divinely guided and Prophetically endorsed language for this act of worship. The Ibrahimi Salawat — named for its invocation of the البركة upon إبراهيم ﷺ — is the most comprehensive and most highly recommended formula, taught by النبي ﷺ himself and incorporated into every Islamic الصلاة as the salawat of the tashahud. The most complete form is: 'Allahumma salli 'ala Muhammadin wa 'ala علي Muhammadin, kama sallayta 'ala Ibrahima wa 'ala علي إبراهيم ﷺ, innaka Hamidun Majid. Allahumma barik 'ala Muhammadin wa 'ala علي Muhammadin, kama barakta 'ala Ibrahima wa 'ala علي إبراهيم ﷺ, innaka Hamidun Majid.' The translation is: 'O الله, send البركات upon محمد ﷺ and upon the الأسرة of محمد ﷺ, as You sent البركات upon إبراهيم ﷺ and upon the الأسرة of إبراهيم ﷺ — verily, You are Praiseworthy and Glorious. O الله, bless محمد ﷺ and the الأسرة of محمد ﷺ, as You blessed إبراهيم ﷺ and the الأسرة of إبراهيم ﷺ — verily, You are Praiseworthy and Glorious.' The theological structure of this formula is remarkable. The supplicant asks الله to bless النبي ﷺ in a manner that parallels His البركة of إبراهيم ﷺ — and إبراهيم ﷺ is specifically chosen لأن he is the most universally honored of all الأنبياء, the progenitor of the Abrahamic religious التراث, and the friend (khalil) of الله. By invoking the البركة upon محمد ﷺ in comparison to that upon إبراهيم ﷺ, the salawat acknowledges النبي ﷺ's supreme prophetic status بينما using the most elevated precedent in the prophetic التراث as the measure of the requested البركة. The divine names used at the close — Hamid (Praiseworthy) and Majid (Glorious) — are specifically chosen for their connection to the name محمد ﷺ (from the root h-m-d, meaning praise) and to the divine majesty that the salawat invokes. Simpler formulas are أيضاً permissible and rewarded, بما في ذلك the brief: 'Salla Allahu 'alayhi wa sallam' (May الله send البركات and peace upon him) — commonly written as SAW or (ﷺ) in English — which is the minimum salawat uttered by المسلمون at every mention of النبي ﷺ's name. Al-Jahdhami notes that بينما the simpler formula is valid and rewarded, the more complete Ibrahimi Salawat carries greater الأجر and should be used at the more formal and deliberate occasions of salawat practice. The العلماء have discussed whether يجوز to use formulas of salawat not explicitly transmitted from النبي ﷺ — formulas composed by later العلماء and Sufi masters — بالإضافة إلى the P
